The **Eisbären Berlin** ([pronunciation](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/1/14/De-Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin.ogg/De-Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin.ogg.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin.ogg "File:De-Eisbären Berlin.ogg"); English: *Berlin Polar Bears*) is a professional [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") team based in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). The team competes in the [Deutsche Eishockey Liga](/wiki/Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga "Deutsche Eishockey Liga") (DEL), the highest level of play in professional German ice hockey, and is also one of the league's founding members. The Eisbären have won the DEL championship more often than any other team, with eleven DEL championships as of the [2024-25 season](/wiki/2024-25_DEL_season "2024-25 DEL season"). They won the [German ice hockey cup](/wiki/German_Cup_(ice_hockey) "German Cup (ice hockey)") in 2008 as well as the [European Trophy](/wiki/European_Trophy "European Trophy") in 2010. Before [reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification") the team won the [East German](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") ice hockey championship 15 times as [SC Dynamo Berlin](/wiki/SC_Dynamo_Berlin "SC Dynamo Berlin").[[1]](#cite_note-History:_All_Teams-1)

The club's origins go back to 1954. It was the ice hockey department of sports club SC Dynamo Berlin. Following incorporation into the West German [1. Bundesliga](/wiki/Eishockey-Bundesliga "Eishockey-Bundesliga") in 1990, the ice hockey department became the independent ice hockey club EHC Dynamo Berlin, and then in 1992 renamed EHC Eisbären Berlin. The home games are played at the [Uber Arena](/wiki/Uber_Arena "Uber Arena").

The Eisbären Berlin are owned by the [Anschutz Entertainment Group](/wiki/Anschutz_Entertainment_Group "Anschutz Entertainment Group"). The official logo of the team is the polar bear, a reference to the bear appearing on [Berlin's coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Berlin "Coat of arms of Berlin").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1954–1994: GDR and 1. Bundesliga years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1954–1994: GDR and 1. Bundesliga years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/DietmarPeters.jpg/250px-DietmarPeters.jpg)](/wiki/File:DietmarPeters.jpg)

[Dietmar Peters](/wiki/Dietmar_Peters "Dietmar Peters") while playing for SC Dynamo Berlin.

The sports club [SC Dynamo Berlin](/wiki/SC_Dynamo_Berlin "SC Dynamo Berlin") was part of the [SV Dynamo](/wiki/SV_Dynamo "SV Dynamo") sports association. The SV Dynamo was the nationwide sports association of the [Stasi](/wiki/Stasi "Stasi"), [Volkspolizei](/wiki/Volkspolizei "Volkspolizei"), and customs. The sports club offered a wide spectrum of competitive athletic activities, including track and field, football, swimming, gymnastics, [fencing](/wiki/Fencing "Fencing"), [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)"), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") and [ice skating](/wiki/Ice_skating "Ice skating"). SC Dynamo Berlin created an ice hockey section in 1954.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-DeutscheEishockey_Meisterschaftenp139-3) The hockey section originally began as an ice hockey section of the [SV Deutsche Volkspolizei Berlin](/wiki/SG_Dynamo_Hohensch%C3%B6nhausen#Ice_hockey "SG Dynamo Hohenschönhausen") sports community in 1950.[[3]](#cite_note-DeutscheEishockey_Meisterschaftenp139-3)

SC Dynamo Berlin trained and played its matches in the large [Werner-Seelenbinder-Halle](/wiki/Werner-Seelenbinder-Halle "Werner-Seelenbinder-Halle") in [Prenzlauer Berg](/wiki/Prenzlauer_Berg "Prenzlauer Berg") during its first years. The team won their first East German title in 1966. The league was reduced to just two teams, SC Dynamo Berlin and [SG Dynamo Weißwasser](/wiki/Lausitzer_F%C3%BCchse "Lausitzer Füchse"), in 1971, which made up the smallest ice hockey league in the world. Altogether, SC Dynamo Berlin won the East German championship 15 times, the last title coming in 1988. In 1984, SC Dynamo Berlin competed at the European Cup of Champions and came in third place.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In 1990, the year of German reunification, both former East German ice hockey clubs, SC Dynamo Berlin and SG Dynamo Weißwasser, which had been renamed [PEV Weißwasser](/wiki/PEV_Wei%C3%9Fwasser "PEV Weißwasser"), were assigned to the 1. Bundesliga, at the time the highest level of play in German ice hockey. The ice hockey department of SC Dynamo Berlin became independent ice hockey club EHC Dynamo Berlin in the same year. However, Berlin was unable to compete successfully and was consequently relegated to the lower 2. Bundesliga at the end of the season. The club was promoted back to the 1. Bundesliga following the 1991–92 season. It was known that the president of SV Dynamo and the head of the Stasi [Erich Mielke](/wiki/Erich_Mielke "Erich Mielke") had been a warm supporter of ice hockey. EHC Dynamo Berlin had been financed by the East German Ministry of the Interior until the end of 1990.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The club tried to distance itself from its East German image, under the leadership of Günter Haake and manager Lorenz Funk.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1992 the club was renamed again, this time to "EHC Eisbären Berlin" and the polar bear logo was also introduced. However, due to severe financial difficulties, the club had to rely heavily on its junior and other low-tier players and thus regularly finished at the bottom of the standings and struggled to avoid relegation to the 2. Bundeliga.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### 1994–present: DEL years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1994–present: DEL years")]

The German Eishockey Bundesliga was abolished following the 1993–94 season and a new league – Deutsche Eishockey Liga (DEL) – was created. The Eisbären are one of the league's 18 founding members. Following the [Bosman ruling](/wiki/Bosman_ruling "Bosman ruling") of 1995, the club replaced almost the entire roster with veteran European players in 1996–97.[[8]](#cite_note-eisbaeren-8) The team finished the regular season in third place and reached the DEL play-offs for the first time. In 1997 the club was renamed EHC neue Eisbären Berlin gegr. 1997 e.V.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The following season, 1997–98, the Eisbären advanced to the final round of the DEL play-offs and finished the season as runner up. The team also participated in the [1998 IIHF Continental Cup](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_IIHF_Continental_Cup "1997–98 IIHF Continental Cup") in [Tampere](/wiki/Tampere "Tampere"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), and finished in second place.[[8]](#cite_note-eisbaeren-8) In 1998–99, Eisbären participated in the [European Hockey League](/wiki/European_Hockey_League "European Hockey League") and finished in third place.[[8]](#cite_note-eisbaeren-8)

The [Anschutz Entertainment Group](/wiki/Anschutz_Entertainment_Group "Anschutz Entertainment Group") acquired sole ownership of the Eisbären in 1999, ensuring financial stability.[[8]](#cite_note-eisbaeren-8) The team finished in second place at the [2000 IIHF Continental Cup](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_IIHF_Continental_Cup "1999–2000 IIHF Continental Cup"). In 2002–03 and 2003–04 the team finished the DEL regular season in first place, but fell short of capturing the title. At that time, the team had already become hugely popular, selling out 28 of 31 home games during the 2003–04 season[[8]](#cite_note-eisbaeren-8) In 2005–06 and 2006–07, the Eisbären were the German representative at the [Spengler Cup](/wiki/Spengler_Cup "Spengler Cup") in [Davos](/wiki/Davos "Davos"), Switzerland.[[8]](#cite_note-eisbaeren-8)

In 2004–05, a season marked by the [2004–05 NHL lockout](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NHL_lockout "2004–05 NHL lockout"), saw the Eisbären capture their first DEL championship. With the help of NHL veterans such as [Erik Cole](/wiki/Erik_Cole "Erik Cole"), [Nathan Dempsey](/wiki/Nathan_Dempsey "Nathan Dempsey"), and [Olaf Kölzig](/wiki/Olaf_K%C3%B6lzig "Olaf Kölzig"), Berlin beat Mannheim in three games.[[8]](#cite_note-eisbaeren-8) The title was defended successfully in 2006 against the [DEG Metro Stars](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorfer_EG "Düsseldorfer EG").[[8]](#cite_note-eisbaeren-8) Their third DEL championship was captured in 2008, when the Eisbären beat [Kölner Haie](/wiki/K%C3%B6lner_Haie "Kölner Haie") in the final round of the play-offs. In 2008, the Eisbären hosted the [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")'s [Tampa Bay Lightning](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Lightning "Tampa Bay Lightning") in a pre-season game, which the Lightning won 4:1.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The fourth DEL title followed in 2009, when the [DEG Metro Stars](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorfer_EG "Düsseldorfer EG") were beaten 3:1 in a best-of-five final play-off round. In 2010, the team won the European Trophy, continuing its success on an international scale. A fifth DEL title was won in 2011, when the [Grizzly Adams Wolfsburg](/wiki/Grizzlys_Wolfsburg "Grizzlys Wolfsburg") were swept in the final round of the play-offs.[[11]](#cite_note-11) A sixth title was won in 2012, after a full-length [best-of-five final](/wiki/Playoff_format "Playoff format") against [Adler Mannheim](/wiki/Adler_Mannheim "Adler Mannheim"), with a seventh coming the next year after a 3:1 series victory over [Kölner Haie](/wiki/K%C3%B6lner_Haie "Kölner Haie").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) With their 2013 title, the Eisbären Berlin had won 7 DEL titles, including three straight, making them the DEL championship record holder for several years; [Adler Mannheim](/wiki/Adler_Mannheim "Adler Mannheim") later tied the record in [2019](/wiki/2018-19_DEL_season "2018-19 DEL season").

In the 2016–17 league season, the Eisbären Berlin drew an average home attendance of 12,052.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Eisbären ultimately won their eighth DEL championship in [2021](/wiki/2020-21_DEL_season "2020-21 DEL season"), defeating [Grizzlys Wolfsburg](/wiki/Grizzlys_Wolfsburg "Grizzlys Wolfsburg") 2:1 in a best-of-three, and in the process reclaiming the record for most DEL championships.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Home arena

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Home arena")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Mercedes-Benz_Arena_Berlin_August_2015.JPG/250px-Mercedes-Benz_Arena_Berlin_August_2015.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mercedes-Benz_Arena_Berlin_August_2015.JPG)

Since 2008, the home ice has been [Uber Arena](/wiki/Uber_Arena "Uber Arena").

SC Dynamo Berlin played its home matches in the [Werner-Seelenbinder-Halle](/wiki/Werner-Seelenbinder-Halle "Werner-Seelenbinder-Halle") in [Prenzlauer Berg](/wiki/Prenzlauer_Berg "Prenzlauer Berg") during the first years.

The team then moved to the large sports complex [Dynamo-Sportforum](/wiki/Sportforum_Hohensch%C3%B6nhausen "Sportforum Hohenschönhausen") in [Hohenschönhausen](/wiki/Alt-Hohensch%C3%B6nhausen "Alt-Hohenschönhausen") in 1964. The team played in the ice hockey facility of the Sportforum Hohenschönhausen until the 2007–08 season. The corrugated roof of the ice hockey facility gave rise to its popular name ”[The Corrugated Palace](/wiki/Wellblechpalast "Wellblechpalast")" ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Wellblechpalast*). The capacity of the facility is 4,695 spectators.

Eisbären Berlin was one of the few former East German ice hockey clubs who managed to establish itself against the competition from clubs from former [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany"). The team beceame popular in areas such as [Hohenschönhausen](/wiki/Hohensch%C3%B6nhausen "Hohenschönhausen"), [Marzahn](/wiki/Marzahn "Marzahn") and [Hellersdorf](/wiki/Hellersdorf "Hellersdorf"). Supporters of Eisbären Berlin chanted "East, East, [East Berlin](/wiki/East_Berlin "East Berlin")" and "Dynamo, Dynamo" well into the 2000s.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Eisbären Berlin moved its home matches to the new [Uber Arena](/wiki/Uber_Arena "Uber Arena") (then known as O2 World) in the 2008–09 season. The Uber Arena is a multi-functional arena located in [Friedrichshain](/wiki/Friedrichshain "Friedrichshain") near the [Spree](/wiki/Spree_(river) "Spree (river)") river. It has a seating-capacity of 14,200.[[19]](#cite_note-19) However, the team still uses the Wellblechpalast as a training facility. The youth teams of Eisbären Berlin are also based in the Sportforum Hohenschönhausen.

The northern curved end of the Uber Arena, with [terracing](/wiki/Terrace_(stadium) "Terrace (stadium)") for active supporters of Eisbären Berlin, was officially named the Hartmut Nickel Curve ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Hartmut Nickel Kurve*) in 2019, after the passing of legendary SC Dynamo Berlin forward [Hartmut Nickel](/wiki/Hartmut_Nickel "Hartmut Nickel").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-BKPapa_Eisbär-21) Nickel was popularly known as "Father Polarbear" ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Papa Eisbär*) in the club.[[21]](#cite_note-BKPapa_Eisbär-21)

## Team anthem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Team anthem")]

The official team anthem of the Eisbären Berlin is "Hey, wir wollen die Eisbären seh'n" (approximately "Hey, We Want to See The Polar Bears"), recorded by veteran East German band the [Puhdys](/wiki/Puhdys "Puhdys") in 1997. The song became a popular tune in German mountain resorts during [après-ski](/wiki/Apr%C3%A8s-ski "Après-ski") parties, and went on to appear on several winter-themed music compilations. Dutch team [Geleen Eaters](/wiki/Geleen_Eaters "Geleen Eaters") have also used a lyrically altered cover of the song as their victory anthem.

The Eisbären [goal song](/wiki/Goal_celebration "Goal celebration") consists of a sequence of four separate elements. The line "Berlin, Halleluja Berlin", from the song "Brandenburg" by Rainald Grebe is followed by the [can-can](/wiki/Can-can "Can-can") from [Jacques Offenbach](/wiki/Jacques_Offenbach "Jacques Offenbach")'s [Orpheus in the Underworld](/wiki/Orpheus_in_the_Underworld "Orpheus in the Underworld") and the line "Ach du meine Nase" by the East German puppet character [Pittiplatsch](/wiki/Pittiplatsch "Pittiplatsch"). The sequence is completed by the children's rhyme "Ene mene miste" from the popular children's TV programme [Rappelkiste](/wiki/Rappelkiste "Rappelkiste").
